changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "The Maebara Manor"

TextENGNew: ``...So Kei-chan, you're not actually some great wealthy person?``
``Where's that coming from? Did I ever show up to school in a luxury car?``
changeReason: Old translates 高級車 as 'limo' (a specific type of vehicle) while new correctly renders it as 'luxury car,' which is the actual meaning of the Japanese term.

TextENGNew: ``How much is your monthly allowance?``
``A thousand yen.``
``My. That's rather ordinary, I must say.``
``...His lunchbox side dishes are ordinary. He isn't wealthy.``
What on earth was this conversation?!
Out of nowhere I'm asked about my allowance, then told I'm ordinary and not wealthy!
``Ahahaha. Sorry, sorry!``
Rena seemed to notice my bewildered expression and laughed cheerfully.
``Your house is enormous, isn't it, Kei-chan? So even while it was being built, all of Hinamizawa was calling it the Maebara Manor and watching it with great interest.``
changeReason: Old misses 建築中から ('since during construction') — the point is that the nickname started while the house was still being built, not just that the architecture itself drew attention.

TextENGNew: The... the Maebara Manor~?!
``With a house that big, everyone was whispering about what kind of wealthy person must live there.``
Ah, I see.@k That explains it.
...The house is undeniably large in stature.@k That sort of misunderstanding was bound to happen.
``My hypothesis is that you spent so much money building the house that you are now quite destitute~!``
changeReason: Old uses third person ('they spent') when Satoko is directly addressing Keiichi; new correctly uses second person ('you spent') matching the JPN dialogue.

TextENGNew: ``...Being poor, how sad, how very sad.``
...Rika-chan patted my head with pity in her eyes.@k I went from being treated as a millionaire to a pauper in an instant...
``Sorry to ruin the fun, but we're neither rich nor poor. An extremely ordinary, average household—nothing more.``
``A house that big cannot be called ordinary! And the entryway is magnificent, and the gate is wide enough for a large vehicle to pass through!!@k That is absolutely not ordinary!``
As if the size of a house were the measure of one's wealth.
changeReason: Old frames it as an accepted saying ('They do say...') but the Japanese (言わんばかりだな) expresses Keiichi's sarcastic observation that they're acting 'as if' house size measures wealth.

TextENGNew: Our house is so large because it includes my father's atelier.
There are multiple workrooms, and his past pieces are displayed all over the place...@k And every single one of them is massive.
With all that, the part of the house the family actually lives in is perhaps a third of the total.
He designed it with the idea of someday hosting a personal exhibition at home, so the layout accommodates the flow of people and vehicles.
...Incidentally, the magnificent entryway Mion mentioned belongs to the atelier side and is normally kept closed.
The entrance the Maebara family actually uses is quite plain and modest.
Appearance and reality are worlds apart, that's all.
changeReason: Old translates this as a literal statement about the house interior versus exterior, but the Japanese (見かけと中身は大違い) is a general observation meaning 'appearances and reality are very different' — a broader philosophical point.

TextENGNew: ``I'd really love to explore your house sometime, Kei-chan. You claim not to be wealthy, yet there's that manor!!@k Just what on earth is hidden inside?!``
``M-Maybe there's something adorable hidden inside! Hau~!``
``I'm sure you've spent all your money and have nothing left for furniture, so it's full of bleak, empty rooms!``
``...If there are carpeted rooms, I would very much like to roll around in them.``
``Wahh~! Yes, yes! Rena wants to roll around too~!``
...Their imaginations were running away with them in very cheerful directions...
Still... in the not-too-distant future, maybe I'd invite everyone over.
...Father has a soft spot for girls, so he might even let them look around the atelier.
The cicadas' cries grew ever more lively, and the sky stretched on without end.
changeReason: Old translates セミ as 'higurashi' (a specific cicada species) when the Japanese uses the generic term for cicadas; new correctly uses 'cicadas.' Also, the old's 'sky towered over everything' misses どこまでも高い ('endlessly high/stretched without end').

TextENGNew: Hot, but crisp—it smelled of early summer.
